content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF

NIELSEN, MERKSAMER,
PARRINELLO, MUELLER & NAYLOR
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

MARIN COUNTY                                770 L STREET, SUITE 800                     SAN FRANCISCO
591 REDWOOD HIGHWAY, #4000                  SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814                650 CALIFORNIA STREET, SUITE 2650
MILL VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 94941               TELEPHONE (916) 446-6752                    SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108
TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800                                                                TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800


                                            April 9, 1992


Ms. Lynda Cassady
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance and Analysis Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

     Re:  Telephone Advice on Lobbyist Employer Reporting

Dear Lynda:

     I am writing to confirm telephone advice which you provided
to me on Wednesday, April 8, 1992.  My request is on behalf of a
number of clients who engage in product development and sales
nationally and internationally.

     My question involves California lobbyist employer reporting
for research conducted as part of product development for market.
For example, assume that a company conducts general product
development research, which includes information about such
issues as product safety, environmental impact, packaging and
market appeal, and the company subsequently uses some of the
results of this research for presentation to various government
regulatory agencies, including California, which might influence
the agencies' regulatory policy.  Would the cost of conducting
the research, which was not initiated with the intent of
influencing administrative regulations, be reportable as an
"other payment to influence" by a California lobbyist employer?

     Your answer was that unless the research was initiated for
the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action,
the background research would not be reportable; only the direct
costs of using the background research for influencing
legislative or administrative action, such as printing special
brochures or preparing charts for presentations to government
officials, would be reportable as other payments to influence.
You cited Regulation 18616(f)(2)(c) as the basis of your
telephone advice.

Ms. Lynda Cassady
April 9, 1992
Page 2


I would appreciate written confirmation of this telephone
advice. Call me at 446-6752 if you have any questions.

Very truly yours,

Bruce W. Robeck

BWR/bl

sections.question: For example, assume that a company conducts general product development research, which includes information about such issues as product safety, environmental impact, packaging and market appeal, and the company subsequently uses some of the results of this research for presentation to various government regulatory agencies, including California, which might influence the agencies' regulatory policy. Would the cost of conducting the research, which was not initiated with the intent of influencing administrative regulations, be reportable as an 'other payment to influence' by a California lobbyist employer?
sections.conclusion: Unless the research was initiated for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action, the background research would not be reportable; only the direct costs of using the background research for influencing legislative or administrative action, such as printing special brochures or preparing charts for presentations to government officials, would be reportable as other payments to influence.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether costs of general product development research that is subsequently used in presentations to government regulatory agencies must be reported as lobbying expenditures under California law.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC advised that research costs are reportable only if the research was initiated with the intent to influence legislative or administrative action; indirect research costs are not reportable, but direct costs of using research results for influencing government (such as presentation materials) are reportable.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming correspondence letter from an attorney requesting written confirmation of prior telephone advice. No formal question/conclusion sections present; question and conclusion extracted from body text. Document represents a request for confirmation rather than an FPPC-generated advice letter response.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: For example, assume that a company conducts general product development research, which includes information about such issues as product safety, environmental impact, packaging and market appeal, and the company subsequently uses some of the results of this research for presentation to various government regulatory agencies, including California, which might influence the agencies' regulatory policy. Would the cost of conducting the research, which was not initiated with the intent of influencing administrative regulations, be reportable as an 'other payment to influence' by a California lobbyist employer?

CONCLUSION: Unless the research was initiated for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action, the background research would not be reportable; only the direct costs of using the background research for influencing legislative or administrative action, such as printing special brochures or preparing charts for presentations to government officials, would be reportable as other payments to influence.

My question involves California lobbyist employer reporting for research conducted as part of product development for market. For example, assume that a company conducts general product development research, which includes information about such issues as product safety, environmental impact, packaging and market appeal, and the company subsequently uses some of the results of this research for presentation to various government regulatory agencies, including California, which might influence the agencies' regulatory policy. Your answer was that unless the research was initiated for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action, the background research would not be reportable; only the direct costs of using the background research for influencing legislative or administrative action, such as printing special brochures or preparing charts for presentations to government officials, would be reportable as other payments to influence. embedding.summary: Attorney Bruce Robeck writes to FPPC consultant Lynda Cassady requesting written confirmation of telephone advice regarding the reportability of product development research costs as lobbying expenditures under California law. FPPC advised that only direct costs of using research to influence government action are reportable, not general background research costs.
